Name: Brock Bowers
Outlook: Who has two thumbs and might be the only legitimate pass-catching option on his team in 2026? This guy! Bowers followed up his incredible TE1 rookie season in 2024 with a TE8 finish in 2025, which, honestly, feels almost just as successful given the state of the QB and organization in Vegas. His targets per game dropped from 9.0 to 7.1, but he broke through with seven TDs in only 12 games played. Add in some competent QB play, either in the form of Kirk Cousins or Fernando Mendoza, and Bowers could rival Trey McBride’s 169 targets of last year. There is so little target competition in Las Vegas that he could break fantasy in 2026.
Dynasty: With the revamping of the Las Vegas offense and Klint Kubiak’s arrival, is it possible that Brock Bowers has gained even more steam as the TE1 in dynasty? There will always be the Bowers/McBride debate, but regardless of which side you land on, Bowers can and should be the cornerstone of your dynasty squad if you are fortunate to have him.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Trey McBride
Outlook: If you were able to behold the TE season to end all TE seasons, you are probably still singing the praises of TMcB. With Jacoby Brissett at the helm, Trey McBride was unlocked, running 696(!) routes while breaking the single-season TE reception record with 126. He took advantage of various injuries among other Cardinals pass catchers to finish the year with 11 receiving TDs, a massive jump from the two he scored the year previous. We know that the TD total stands quite a chance to regress on a Cardinals team projected to win the fewest games in the NFL. Be that as it may, expect another year of massive volume, giving fantasy managers a positional advantage.
Dynasty: What is there to say? Enjoy. McBride signed a four-year extension with the Cardinals in April 2025, so he is not going anywhere soon. McBride and Bowers will dance back and forth as TE1/TE2 for many years to come.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Tyler Warren
Outlook: The beginning of the year was an utter delight for Warren as he produced just one fantasy finish outside the top-12 in the first seven weeks, thanks to the resurgence of Daniel Jones. He struggled near the end of the year, along with the rest of the Colts’ offense, but still posted 817 yards receiving and a TE7 finish on the year. The four receiving TDs felt on the lower side, but this could go up with the loss of Michael Pittman Jr. in 2026. Warren was the TE3 when it came to route participation at 83%, and we don’t see that going down any time soon. He is in that second tier of TEs in redraft that can be fantasy difference-makers even with the uncertain recovery timeline of Jones.
Dynasty: Anyone who went all-in on Tyler Warren last year is extremely thankful and confident that they have their TE position locked up for a good while. His 21% target share should stay consistent as he will be the cornerstone of the Colts’ passing attack for seasons to come. Enjoy!
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Colston Loveland
Outlook: Oh, Mr. Loveland. Slow clap for the managers that pounced on Loveland quickly when they saw the promise of a breakout. His Week 17 performance against San Francisco was one that fantasy championships were made of. We were nervous with the myriad of weapons in Chicago and how Ben Johnson’s offense would come together, but Loveland was the clear first read in the offense into the NFL playoffs. DJ Moore has left town, and Johnson proved that what he did in Detroit was not a fluke, so ranking Loveland in the top four seems 100% valid. We cannot wait to see the jump Loveland can make this season.
Dynasty: Loveland had at least 15% of the team’s targets in each of the final nine games in 2025, and you know we love a TE that gets better as the season progresses. Loveland is in the second tier of dynasty TEs and is either TE3 or TE4 in start-up rankings. Congratulations if he is on your squad, and enjoy the ride with one of the youngest and most valuable players at the position.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Harold Fannin Jr.
Outlook: We were uncertain about how it would all play out in the TE room with David Njoku still the de facto TE1, but Fannin came out of the gate hard in 2025. He was the bright spot in Cleveland last season, leading the team in targets (107), receptions (72), receiving yards (731), and receiving TDs (six) all as a rookie with a carousel of QBs throwing him the ball. The offense in Cleveland was abhorrent, but Fannin still managed to join Sam LaPorta as one of two rookie TEs since 2000 with 70/700/6. He now has to compete with rookies KC Concepcion and Denzel Boston for targets, but the offense on the whole looks to have much more potential with Todd Monken driving the bus. He finished as the TE5 in 2025, and he should go around that in redraft, especially with the departure of Njoku.
Dynasty: Njoku was released by the Browns in March, clearing the way for Fannin to be their TE1 of the future on a team that looks poised to steadily improve. He is a top-5 dynasty TE, in the bottom of the second tier, just below Colston Loveland, and Tyler Warren. If you drafted him in your rookie drafts in 2025, congratulations on quite a value.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Sam LaPorta
Outlook: LaPorta tried real hard to put together a season reminiscent of his astounding rookie year starting out fairly well with four top-6 finishes before being sidelined with a season-ending back injury. His 12.2 yards per reception were a career high, and his 81% catch percentage is delightful. But what happens now? New OC Drew Petzing catapulted an all-time season from Trey McBride in Arizona so we are left wondering if LaPorta will get the TMcB treatment this season. The Lions have one of the easiest schedules in the NFL making LaPorta’s later round ADP palatable as we still consider his ceiling and TD upside enticing.
Dynasty: LaPorta is heading into the final year of his rookie deal, and if he ends 2026 with a bounce back and a bang, expect the team to lock him up. His underwhelming 2025 season and back issues do present an opportunity to buy at a discount. He is a solid TE6-TE8 in dynasty startups.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Tucker Kraft
Outlook: Kraft was on his way to a monster season in 2025, boasting two TE1 weeks and averaging close to six targets a game, until he tore his ACL in Week 9 against Carolina. He was Green Bay’s leading receiver with 32 catches for 489 yards and six TDs on 44 targets up until that point. Despite his major injury, you will still have to pay up to draft Kraft, making him a dicey draft decision. The talent is there, and the targets could be even easier to come by, but also factor in that Kraft might not be ready to go by Week 1.
Dynasty: Of course, Kraft is ahead of schedule and reported to be back early in the regular season, targeting a Week 1 return. Shocking. 2026 marks the final year of his deal with the Packers, but GM Brian Gutekunst has made it clear that talks regarding a contract extension are underway. Both Romeo Doubs and Dontayvion Wicks have moved on, bumping Kraft up in the target pecking order, and the Green Bay offense should continue to produce at a high level with Jordan Love at the helm. Kraft is a YAC king and a great option if you want to wait in a startup past the big TE names.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Kyle Pitts Sr.
Outlook: Ah, yes, the TE2 last season! As you may remember, Pitts had an incredible final third of the season with four weeks inside the top-10, including his 40-point(!) Week 15 blowout game against Tampa Bay. We are not expecting this from Pitts heading into 2026, so make sure to let your league mates reach for him come draft day. Rankings-wise, he is living in that TE6-TE10 range where players can become almost interchangeable. Aside from Bijan Robinson and Drake London, the Falcons do not have much target competition, so a world exists where he could bolster his consistency and build on his 2025 season. The QB situation and a new offensive play-caller bring more risk and uncertainty than we would like.
Dynasty: Pitts signed his franchise tag, giving the Falcons another year to make a decision on a player they took fourth overall ahead of JA’MARR CHASE! That move (among others) cost this Atlanta front office their jobs and likely sunk your dynasty team for years. The man turns 26 this year, and many of us are hoping he heads to a different team after this season with an opportunity for a killer second act.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Travis Kelce
Outlook: Quietly the TE4 in 2025, Kelce finished as the TE8 or better every year as a starter, reminding us he is a true fantasy football legend. Louder for the people in the back! He had a solid 851 yards receiving (TE4) with 584 routes run (TE2), and you can continue to expect to see him on the field as Patrick Mahomes’ good ole security blanket. We would like to see that TD total go up a bit - Kelce had five last year - but to be fair, no one on the team caught more than that number. He is a solid later-round pick if you wait on the TE position, knowing this is his latest ADP in more than a decade. Respect your elders.
Dynasty: The “will-he-or-won’t-he” retire shenanigans continued in the off-season, as Swifties watched to see if Travis would return for one last ride. He signed a one-year, fully guaranteed $12 million deal in March, so expect him to give it his all. That won’t necessarily equate to high-end fantasy success as you ride off into the sunset with the all-timer.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Brenton Strange
Outlook: In his third year in the league, Strange was given the keys to the Jacksonville TE room with the departure of Evan Engram. There was a clear connection with Trevor Lawrence, as Strange had a 77% catch rate, including five weeks as TE10 or better.  Unfortunately, he was out for five weeks due to injury, and now the team double-dipped in the TE well with rookies Nate Boerkircher and Tanner Koziol. We are fairly high on the Jags offense and the value of Trevor Lawrence this year, making Strange a potential cheap late-round pick. If you have a deeper bench, Strange might be worth a shot to see how the team looks out of the gate.
Dynasty: 2026 is the final year of Strange’s rookie deal, which makes his dynasty value dicey when we couple it with Jacksonville’s investment in two TEs in the draft this year. Strange is a hold in dynasty, but who knows what he will be in 2027.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Jake Ferguson
Outlook: The offensive weapons for Dallas were a delight for fantasy managers last season, simply because the defense was nonexistent. Fergalicious took advantage, especially in Weeks 3 through 6 when CeeDee Lamb was out finishing as the TE4, TE5, TE2, and TE10 during those weeks. He set career highs in receptions (82) and TDs (8), but the contributions fell off in the second half. The trained eye will say Ferguson will be due for some TD regression. However, you can trust in the fact that the Dallas D won’t be fixed overnight, making Fergy-Ferg a late-round target who should still get a fair amount of dump-offs in this offense.
Dynasty: Ferguson signed a four-year extension in 2025, making him a big piece of the TE room in Dallas for a hot minute. Competition-wise, the ship looks to have sailed on the Schoon-man (Luke Schoonmaker). We are uncertain if Brevyn Spann-Ford is an actual guy, and the chance of rookie Michael “Trigg Daddy” staying focused enough at practice to make the team is slim to none. All in all, Ferguson looks to be the TE1 on a high-scoring offense for several years. He has a nice and dependable floor for your dynasty squad.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Mark Andrews
Outlook: With Isaiah Likely signing with the New York Giants, all of a sudden, Mark Andrews has life again. The TE19 finish of last season was far from extraordinary, especially as he bottomed out over the final month of the season. Expected TD regression came as well, dropping from 11 to five. Woof. The Ravens didn’t add any huge names TE-wise in the draft - apologies, Matthew Hibner and Josh Cuevas - so the runway exists for Andrews to have a bounce-back season, especially if he can keep up with his red zone target share. He was close to 30% last season.
Dynasty: The Ravens pulled the wool over our eyes last season, teasing the departure of Andrews and opening up the path for Likely’s usage. Snip, snap! Gotcha! It was Likely who was gone from the roster and Andrews who signed a three-year, $39 million extension in December 2025. Andrews looks to still have value on your dynasty squad, whether for a “win now” team, or as trade value if you are rebuilding.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: George Kittle
Outlook: Oh, George. Times are tough. It is hard to get behind an almost 33-year-old TE heading into his 10th NFL season who is coming off an Achilles injury. If anyone can do it, you know it is Kittle; he has given us six seasons where he has finished as the TE5 or higher! The physical freak who has publicly said he is targeting an early-season return could be a value in fantasy drafts, if he drops far enough. Pair him with a vanilla-reliable TE that you can use confidently in the first half of your season, knowing you can pop Kittle on your IR. If he comes back even close to his past form late in the season, you have some options in your playoff push. We don’t suggest even entertaining the option unless you have multiple IR spots.
Dynasty: If you have Kittle in dynasty, you are holding as you would only be getting pennies on the dollar if you trade him. You might get lucky to obtain another solid year from him in 2027 post-Achilles, and after that, it might just be a “thank you so much for your service” farewell.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Oronde Gadsden
Outlook: Fifth-round draft pick Gadsden broke all the Chargers' rookie TE records last year as he finished with 49 receptions on 69 targets, 664 yards receiving, and three TDs. However, keep in mind that 241 of those yards came in two weeks. It was looking like the potential was high for 2026 with the addition of OC Mike McDaniel, but then the team had to go and sign David Njoku. Throw in blocking TE Charlie Kolar and FB Alec Ingold, and Gadsden’s path to fantasy relevance in redraft this year has gotten circuitous at best. Despite the alluring upside, 2026 feels like a tough route for Gadsden to be counted on in fantasy.
Dynasty: We were all certain we had hit fantasy gold in dynasty when Gadsden posted TE1 and TE4 finishes in back-to-back weeks last season. Alas, we flew too close to the sun as he dropped back to Earth faster than a paper straw in a milkshake. The team added David Njoku, but the silver lining is that he only signed a one-year deal, and it could easily be a one-and-done. Gadsden represents an intriguing buy-low opportunity for a team that won’t be depending on him for at least a year.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Chig Okonkwo
Outlook: Chig had his moments in Tennessee, flashing some YAC-ability but never quite being a consistent fantasy threat. He signed a three-year contract with the Washington Commanders, breathing new life into his fantasy prospects on a roster that desperately needs another pass-catcher opposite WR Terry McLaurin. You won’t have to pay too much to take a shot on the guy, so why not? He looks like an easy stash or waiver wire pickup early in the season. Chig is not dead for fantasy yet, we say!
Dynasty: We saw flashes in a sub-par Tennessee offense last year with four top-12 weeks in fantasy. As the presumed TE1 in a much better offense, he saw a value boost this offseason but remains a mid-level TE2 for fantasy. If you are looking to add TE depth this off-season for a small price, Chig is your man!
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Dalton Schultz
Outlook: “Dr. Schultz” definitely took some sabbatical weeks in 2025 - clocking eight weeks where he finished as the TE20 or worse. Ick. But then he also had some weeks where he was the dude, slicing and dicing in the operating room, finishing as the TE11 or better seven times. It was tough to anticipate what version of Schultz we were going to get in 2025, making starting him every week a scary decision. A lot will ride on whether Stroud can return to that magical rookie season production. He has had at least 85 targets in the past six seasons, but TDs have never seemed to be his thing. Schultz is a low-ceiling, later-round option in redraft this season.
Dynasty: The man signed a one-year extension in March of this year. He will turn 30 this year and will just be a boring, vanilla TE2 sitting on your dynasty roster, reminding you once in a while that he can find the end zone.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Dallas Goedert
Outlook: The TE3(!) in 2025 returns to Philly with the talented rookie Eli Stowers breathing down his neck. After a league-leading 11 TDs last season - the same as Trey McBride, on half as many receiving yards (591) - we can expect some TD regression. It will be tough to start him every week in redraft, especially with all of the offensive options in Philly.
Dynasty: Goedert signed a one-year extension in the off-season, and with the addition of Stowers, we can see a world where the team moves on after 2026. We can hope for a landing spot where he is featured near the goal line so we can squeak out another year or two in dynasty. He should go off draft boards in the early 20-something range in start-ups.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Dalton Kincaid
Outlook: After his rookie season finish of TE11 in 2023, it has been downhill ever since for the guy we were hoping to become Josh Allen’s No. 1 target. His 49 targets only translated to 571 yards, five TDs, and a TE15 finish. With Joe Brady stepping in as HC, the offense should look similar, but there is no guarantee. The team added DJ Moore in the offseason to finally give Allen a WR1, and TE Dawson Knox continues to be another solid receiving option; remember, everybody eats in Buffalo. You won’t feel confident heading into this season with Kincaid as the only TE on your roster as four targets per game will not get it done in the TE fantasy streets.
Dynasty: There was a time when we were pumped for the young TE tied to a high-powered offense and a QB with an arm who could sling it. He is under contract for two more seasons sustaining his value as a fringe TE1 for dynasty. Keep the light on for Kincaid to see how the offense is run with Brady at the helm, but make that light small. And dimmable.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Hunter Henry
Outlook: Henry was quietly the TE6 on a team where he was second in targets with 87, just behind Stefon Diggs with 102. The problem with Henry was that you never seemed to know when to start him, as he surpassed eight fantasy points per game in less than half of his games (47%). Henry’s weekly uncertainty might not improve much with the addition of AJ Brown and Romeo Doubs. Henry seems to consistently be undervalued in drafts, making him an easy trigger to pull at the end or a pickup off waivers as your second TE on a deep roster.
Dynasty: Henry is a free agent after the 2026 season, and New England drafted Notre Dame rookie Eli Raridon in the third round. They seem to be looking to the future at the position. Henry will be playing his 10th season in 2026, so you probably won’t get much in a trade for him. Hold on and see if you can use him in prime matchups.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Kenyon Sadiq
Outlook: Sadiq’s impressive athletic profile (remember when he broke almost every record at the NFL Combine?) catapulted him into the first round, heading to the New York Jets at pick 16. The question marks related to his usage at Oregon remain, as fantasy managers must question if a man who couldn’t top 600 receiving yards during his final year in college will be successful as a Jet with Geno Smith under center? He could end up second or third on the team in targets behind WR Garrett Wilson and RB Breece Hall, while competing with fellow rookie Omar Cooper Jr. Oh, and there is also a feline in Mason Taylor in the room that Sadiq has to overtake on the depth chart. Between you and me, a dawg always wins over a cat. You probably aren’t counting on Sadiq right away in redraft this year, but his time will eventually come.
Dynasty: Dynasty is another story for Sadiq, who shows strong promise looking into the future. One of the youngest at the position, only 20 years old, and drafted to an organization trying desperately to be “one QB away,” Sadiq could be one of the great TEs of the next few years. Some managers may be down to overpay, wowed by his Combine athletic performance, evidenced by him going as high as the 1.07 in rookie drafts. However, you might be ok waiting for someone like Eli Stowers several picks later and get a similar ROI. However you cut it, Sadiq’s value for dynasty is strong and should hold there for a while.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Isaiah Likely
Outlook: Remember Week 1 of the 2024 season when Likely had over 100 yards receiving and a TD, and the entire fantasy community went, wait whaaaat? Mhmm. The man is now out of Mark Andrews’ shadow and ready to live large in New York. Target-wise, the big competition is Malik Nabers, and behind him are the likes of Darius Slayton, Darnell Mooney, and fellow TE Theo Johnson.  We are following the money ($26 million guaranteed) and his connection with John Harbaugh in assuming that Likely will be a significant part of this offense. You don’t have to go after him early to invest in this late-round flier.
Dynasty: Likely’s dynasty situation really couldn’t be much better. He lands on a new team with a coach who pushed for him and shares rapport with. The 25-year-old signed a three-year deal, and he has an exciting young QB in Jaxson Dart to grow with. He does have Theo Johnson sharing the room, but Harbaugh has made clear his proclivity for multiple TEs on the field. If you have Likely, hold and hope for the breakout.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: T.J. Hockenson
Outlook: After three TE4 or better finishes in three out of four years, Hockenson fell and fell hard. He saw 66 mostly forgettable targets in 2025. He was unstartable almost every week until he tapped out for the final two weeks of the season due to a shoulder injury. The Vikings’ offense will look different this season with either Kyler or “Nine” under center, and with Justin Jefferson and Jordan Addison still there, it is hard to consider him in redraft this season, at least until you see how the usage pans out.
Dynasty: Hockenson used to be a fantastic dynasty asset as recently as a couple of years back, but oh, how quickly the tides turn! He is almost 29, and even though there is no one threatening him in the depth chart, he seems to have lost his shine. If Kyler can bring him back to his former glory, consider yourself lucky.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Juwan Johnson
Outlook: There have been whispers in the bushes every year about a potential Juwan Johnson breakout season, and we finally got it in 2025! His TE10 finish was the best of his career on the back of 529 routes run, the fifth most for the position. It was the rapport with QB Tyler Shough that really greased the gears, and they should be ready to come out firing to start the season. The team added offensive weapons with eighth-overall pick Jordyn Tyson, Travis Etienne Jr., and TEs Oscar Delp and Noah Fant, which likely eats into his target volume. Going off draft boards past TE15 territory means you can shoot your shot with the former basketballer if you had a fun ride last year.
Dynasty: We do not usually suggest targeting almost 30-year-old TEs with a classic sixth-year breakout. The team restructured Johnson’s contract this spring, giving themselves an out after 2027. Add in the drafting of Georgia’s Oscar Delp, and it suggests that the team is preparing for a world without Juwan-son as a Saint. If you can capitalize on a strong 2025 and trade him to a TE-needy team, we don’t think it is a bad decision. If no takers, you are holding and hoping.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Pat Freiermuth
Outlook: Is the Muth still Luth? Unfortunately, the answer might be no with only 486 yards receiving and four TDs in 2025. The team gets back the one and only Aaron Rodgers, who had 67% of his passing yardage come after the catch, the highest rate in the NFL. With Mike Tomlin and Jonnu Smith out and Mike McCarthy and Michael Pittman Jr. in, it is up in the air how this offense will function. Look, it’s ok to just say no in fantasy.
Dynasty: It is perfectly fine if you want nothing to do with this Pittsburgh offense, but keep in mind that this is probably the last year of Aaron Rodgers (we hope). The Mike McCarthy era has now begun. The Muth signed a four-year extension in 2024, so he will be a Steeler through 2028. He is a hold as we hope for a team makeup that lets the Muth get Luth again, as he did in 2022 and 2024.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Cade Otton
Outlook: All hail the ultimate cardio king! Otton ran the fourth most routes among TEs in 2025 (540!) and was on the field for the third most passing plays (625!). Obviously, he crushed it in fantasy football, right? RIGHT? Exactly. Otton finished the season with only one TD, reminding us that fantasy football and real-life football regularly do not align. On a team full of offensive weapons, including two solid pass-catching RBs, Otton’s time in the sun is almost set. But hey, if your league gets fantasy points for getting your steps in, Otton should be the first off the board.
Dynasty: You aren’t going to get anything of value for Cade Otton if you try to trade him, so on your bench, he shall live as you dream at night of positive TD regression. If somehow that happens, ship him out the door faster than Jason cranking down the AC in July.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: AJ Barner
Outlook: With Noah Fant out of the picture, the TE position was up for grabs in Seattle, and Barner did a decent job showing he should be the guy moving forward. Finishing as the TE14, he had a decent 64 targets, which turned into 519 yards and six TDs. His 18 looks in the red zone were stellar too, just two fewer than JSN. He remains too volatile in redraft, especially with fellow Seahawk Elijah Arroyo still in the mix. Barner had finishes as high as TE1 and as low as TE58 last year. It would be tough to stomach the amount of Tums we would need to ride that roller coaster.
Dynasty: Barner carries a little more weight in dynasty. He is only 24 and will be with Seattle at least through 2027, a very well-run franchise. However, the same issues exist in dynasty as they do in redraft: four targets a game are just not enough to crack your starting lineup. His seven "TE1" finishes might lure someone into this TE trap, but as we have established on the podcast, a top-12 finish at this position is an outdated term.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Colby Parkinson
Outlook: After five seasons of “who’s that guy” in Los Angeles, 2025 turned into “hey, I know that guy” for Colby Parkinson. He set career highs in targets (56), yards (408), and TDs (8!!). The TDs are certainly an outlier, but he was a certifiable red zone weapon, seeing 22 red zone targets inside the 20, just three shy of Puka Nacua. We are paying attention, Mr. Parkinson. The only problem is  Sean McVay loves TEs, and with a crowded room, you won’t know what weeks to start Parkinson or any of the other Rams TEs.
Dynasty: Good ole Colby is 27 and in the last year of his deal. The Rams have made it known they have their eyes on the future, drafting Terrance Ferguson last year and Max Klare in 2026. You can hold onto him to see if he heads to a team that features him more, and if he doesn’t, there are worse offenses to be tied to.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: David Njoku
Outlook: Njoku is joining the Chargers on a one-year deal, and he joins a room with the new hotness, Oronde Gadsden, and blocking TE Charlie Kolar. Njoku was quickly usurped by rookie Harold Fannin Jr. in Cleveland last season, so it is unclear how he will be used in Mike McDaniel’s offense. You need to keep an eye on how the team functions and read the tea leaves in case you need to grab him from waivers early in the season.
Dynasty: Njoku turns 30 this year and is coming off a knee injury that robbed him of the final four games of the 2025 season. If someone is offering up something for him trade-wise after he has a decent game, it might be time to send Njoku away with a thank-you note and a box of chocolates.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Mike Gesicki
Outlook: Remember those magical “Sticky Gesicki” weeks a few seasons back? Never forget. Gesicki’s great weeks in 2024 primarily made their appearances when Tee Higgins was out of the lineup, and then in 2025, things got even more confusing with QB Joe Burrow missing nine weeks. Gesicki only had a 9.4% target share last season and will always be left battling on that front with talents like Ja’Marr Chase and Higgins on the field. If he is on your roster, you are only entertaining starting him if one of those WRs is out, and for a guy who only had two receiving TDs in 2025, you don’t need that nonsense.
Dynasty: There is so much that has to go a certain way for Gesicki to be relevant in dynasty. Sure, he might be the guy to start on the third Sunday of October when the moon is in its waning phase, and the wind is blowing at exactly 2.2 mph, but c’mon, who has the time for that? The team has more youth on their side with Erick All Jr. (25) and rookie Jack Endries (22), making it even easier to leave Gesicki on your bench.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Greg Dulcich
Outlook: You may have forgotten about the curly-locked, marvelous-mustached Greg D, especially since he has done close to nothing fantasy-wise during his four years so far in the NFL. The best we saw from him was a TE30 finish in his rookie season in Denver. However, Greg showed glimmers of promise at the end of last season finishing second in yards per route run among TEs from Week 9 onward. Miami desperately needs pass-catchers considering a WR core of Malik Washington, Jalen Tolbert, and Tutu Atwell in South Florida. No offense to those guys, but it puts Dulcich in a great position to potentially be the target leader for new QB Malik Willis. Greg D also finished second among all TEs in YAC/catch, only behind Tucker Kraft. He is a name to monitor throughout camp as he potentially could be an early season waiver pickup.
Dynasty: If Greg D takes advantage of this season in Miami fantasy-wise, you may have some trade bait on your hands. There is little chance that the Dolphins don’t look to flesh out this WR room in the upcoming drafts. If he has some spike weeks and you have other competent TEs on your roster, it is worth kicking the tires to see what you could get.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Eli Stowers
Outlook: The consensus TE2 behind Sadiq heading into the NFL Draft is now connected to one of the NFC perennial powers. After a final year at Vandy, where he won the 2025 John Mackey award as college football’s top TE, Stowers' production profile was quite strong, leading the class in targets per route run. Stowers will be listed as a TE, but he is essentially a big hybrid WR/TE who actually began college as a QB. He will be behind Dallas Goedert on the depth chart, making him hard to roster in redraft in 2026 outside of an injury.
Dynasty: 31-year-old Goedert looks to be at the end of his storied career, and when he moves on, Stowers will slide right into a great opportunity. Saquon’s sun may also be setting soon as the team's aged identity looks set to transition. Stowers will be a big part of their future, and if you were lucky enough to snag him in dynasty, he could be the answer to your TE position for a significant amount of time. We have him ranked as a top-12 dynasty TE asset to start 2026.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Cole Kmet
Outlook: The offensive weapons in Chicago are plenty, and Kmet tried desperately to contribute in 2025. Unfortunately, the 347 receiving yards on 48 targets - although helpful for the NFL team - were not helpful for our fantasy squads. We all know that Colston Loveland is the present and future for this franchise, so any hope of using Kmet in redraft is out the window, barring an injury. And don’t you dare put that into the universe!
Dynasty: There was dynasty chatter about Kmet heading into the 2024 season, coming off the heels of back-to-back TE7 finishes. We know those days are over. With Mr. Loveland at the TE helm, Chicago added rookie Sam Roush in the third round so we could see Kmet heading to a new home soon. You can hold on to him to see if he lands on a team that can reignite his offensive spark. He is only 27.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Gunnar Helm
Outlook: There was mixed chatter around Helm as he started his NFL career with the downtrodden Titans. He was the team's TE2 behind our pinup boy Chig (learning from the best, amirite?), and he put together a sneaky decent season. He had three TE11 finishes or better, and this was with a QB who was sacked 55 (!) times and had the third-worst 59% completion rate. Helm’s usage increased after his Week 10 bye, going from 8.7% target share to 13.1%. With Chig Okonkwo heading to the Commanders, the opportunity is there, making him an interesting prospect to keep an eye on, especially in deeper leagues.
Dynasty: Helm is worth more in dynasty, especially with the rebuilding of the Titans. HC Robert Saleh has the reins now, and the organization splurged, taking WR Carnell Tate fourth overall, and padding the WR room with Wan’Dale Robinson. If QB Cam Ward can take a leap, there is a strong chance Helm will be along for the ride, making him an intriguing and somewhat affordable buy if you believe in the team.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Evan Engram
Outlook: When Engram went to Denver in 2025, fantasy managers were hopeful that Sean Payton might have found his new Joker, but the role never really panned out. Engram set a career low in 2025 with 28.8 yards per game and finished the season with only one receiving TD, leaving us wondering if the TE6 and TE2 finishes of a few years prior were the outliers. Do not get your hopes up too much – the team traded for Jaylen Waddle in the offseason, added rookie Justin Joly in the draft, and, the most important part of the puzzle, they still have Adam Trautman. Targets will be even tougher to command, and you will never know when to start him - just remember the good times.
Dynasty: Engram signed a two-year, $16.5 million guaranteed contract with Denver in 2025, giving his dynasty managers a spark of hope, but after his disappointing season, his value in dynasty has dropped drastically. The man turns 32 this year, so anything he gives you from here on out will be a delightful surprise.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Dawson Knox
Outlook: It is super cool being tied to Josh Allen and a high-flying offense, but neither is enough to push Knox into the draftable range in redraft. It has been a good long while since his TE8 season of 2021. Never forget.
Dynasty: You can’t drop that guy because you know what happens if one or two guys go down to injury ahead of him. Find him a cozy corner on your bench, knowing Knox is under contract for two more years in Buffalo as Josh Allen’s best bud.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Mason Taylor
Outlook: Meow. Would you be shocked to find out that Mason Taylor led the Jets in targets in 2025? Me. Too. The main reason was that Garrett Wilson was shut down early due to injury, and there was truly nothing else in town offensively. The second round selection in 2025 is immediately trounced by the drafting of athletic freak TE Kenyon Sadiq. Find Taylor a patch of sunshine and let him curl up. He does not belong in fantasy.
Dynasty: The man with zero broken tackles might have a short tenure in New York as the organization seemingly drafted his replacement, Sadiq, with the 16th overall pick in 2026. You don’t need to hold onto this kitty in dynasty when a dawg is in town.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Michael Mayer
Outlook: Always the bridesmaid, never the bride. Mayer will be the TE2 as long as Brock Bowers is a Raider. But wait, you say…Mayer must have tons of value when Bowers is out of the lineup, right? RIGHT? He did give you one TE5 week when Bowers was sidelined last year, but during the other week, he was TE41. This former 2nd round NFL draft pick will be valuable in 12-personnel sets to the Raiders but not for fantasy.
Dynasty: The majority of Mayer’s dynasty value went poof the moment that Bowers was drafted, and he has done nothing to build it back up. The blocking TE in Las Vegas need not be on your fantasy roster.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Luke Musgrave
Outlook: The Packers double-dipped in the TE in the 2023 NFL Draft, and even though Tucker Kraft has had the breakout season, it was Musgrave whom the team took earlier (second round). Musgrave had a chance to show why he was drafted earlier when Kraft went down with his ACL injury in Week 9, but he did nothing with the opportunity. He averaged just over two targets a game from Week 10 on, only marginally better than the 1.25 a week he was averaging with Kraft on the field. Either way you slice it, not great.
Dynasty: Musgrave’s dynasty window is only slightly propped open as he enters the final year of his rookie deal. Unable to capitalize on Kraft’s missing time in 2025, it is hard to believe that he can do it while sharing time with him in 2026.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Theo Johnson
Outlook: Johnson was putting together a decent sophomore season in 2025, highlighted by an average of 4.9 targets a game and a budding connection with Jaxson Dart. However, he missed the final two weeks of the season with an unspecified illness, ending the year with a whimper. All was looking promising until John Harbaugh came to New York and brought Isaiah Likely with him. Now Johnson is the TE2, dropping out of the redraft conversation. What a roller coaster.
Dynasty: It is frustrating with Johnson, as it feels like the promise of a future TE1 spot on his team was pulled out from under him with the signing of Likely. He is still young and with the Giants on his rookie deal through 2027. We can hope that Harbaugh will utilize more two-TE sets in New York, as he did in Baltimore. Consider Johnson a hold in dynasty, but his window is likely gone. (See what we did there :)
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Terrance Ferguson
Outlook: Ferg-Daddy 2.0 is one of many TEs in Sean McVay’s crowded TE room, including the likes of Tyler Higbee, Colby Parkinson, and rookie Max Klare. We had high hopes for him in his rookie season. He didn’t deliver, running the fourth fewest routes among the team’s TEs and gaining only a 5% target share.  Ferguson did show off via the deep ball as he was the league leader among TEs in deep targets (20+ yards) with 15. Tyler Higbee and Davante Adams are not getting any younger, so perhaps we see a patented second-year breakout from the Oregon TE.
Dynasty: A young TE tied to a prolific offense run by a guy who loves to use multiple TEs? Ferguson sounds like someone worth holding on to, at least for a couple of years, to see what he turns into. He has not played his way into weekly starter status, but hey, Colby Parkinson has definitely given us some startable weeks. Just saying.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Ja'Tavion Sanders
Outlook: Sanders is recovering from a broken fibula and will be battling with Tommy Tremble for the top option at the TE position in Carolina this season. You don’t really want a TE on a team with a QB who averaged under 200 yards passing a week, do you? Do you?
Dynasty: Inconsistent target volume makes you nervous holding onto Sanders in dynasty. The team could move on from Tremble after 2026, so you could tentatively hold onto Sanders to see how the depth chart plays out. You would really need Bryce Young to take a step forward to give you any sense of confidence in Sanders.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Elijah Arroyo
Outlook: With only a 7.7% target share and 15 catches on 26 targets, Arroyo is clearly the TE2 behind AJ Barner on the Super Bowl-winning squad. The team had the third fewest pass attempts in the NFL in 2025, so you aren’t investing in the TE2 in redraft this year.
Dynasty: Arroyo is young and talented, and TEs often do take time to find their footing, so keeping him at the end of your deep bench is understandable. If you have a better use for that roster spot, though, no hard feelings.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Noah Gray
Outlook: As much as we like to admit that Travis Kelce will never retire, the man is cake tasting and choosing floral arrangements. The end is near, but is Gray the next man up? Mahomes has never trusted Gray enough to give him more than one season of 49 targets. He will pop up for a TD here and there, but even in the deepest of TE premium leagues, he is not roster-worthy.
Dynasty: The team liked Gray enough to give him a three-year extension in 2024, but not enough to give him more than three red zone targets in all of 2025. You can hold him due to the fact that he is a backup on a team that is always a bet to make it to the Super Bowl, but know that you are expecting close to nothing from the man.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Darnell Washington
Outlook: Big Darnell (6’7”) continues to be one of the best run blocking TEs in football, but he has very little to offer when it comes to fantasy production in redraft. You are not even looking his way without an injury to the Muth, but even then, would it be anything but cursory? It is cool to just say no, thank you, and slowly walk away.
Dynasty: We all wanted Gigantor 2.0 to be a thing, but with only one top-10 TE finish last season, it is hard to justify Washington as anything more than a bench clogger. Feel free to cut him and hold on to someone who has slightly more ceiling potential. We advise looking for something more than the split-level mid-century one that Pittsburgh is currently offering.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Tommy Tremble
Outlook: Quick, name the best TE on the Carolina Panthers roster. Exactly! On paper, Tommy “High-T” Tremble looks to be the TE1, but with 249 yards receiving and two TDs in 2025, you have to look twice: he is a blocking-first TE. With Ja’Tavion Sanders and Mitchell Evans rounding out this ragtag bunch of hooligans, if you are asked at your draft which Carolina TE you should go for this season, shake your head disappointingly and respond, “None, thank you.”
Dynasty: If you are keeping an eye on one TE in Carolina, it is probably going to be Sanders, who is 23 years old, heading into his third season. Tremble will be 26 this year, and with five seasons under his belt with no real fantasy goodness, you really do not need to worry about him, even in dynasty.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Max Klare
Outlook: Before transferring to Ohio State for his final year, Klare was a production monster at Purdue and showcased target-earning ability that could intrigue at the next level. Unfortunately, his first-year prospects are dim as he was selected by the Rams, who collect TEs like it is going out of style. Los Angeles has Colby Parkinson, Tyler Higbee, Terrance Ferguson, and Davis Allen on the roster, making the rookie just another guy in the room. Sean McVay has shown a strong proclivity to running 13-personnel, so we know Klare will be rotated in.
Dynasty: We think that Klare can eventually prove to be a valuable part of this McVay high-flying offense. He is a great stash in dynasty to see how he is used, but do not expect too much, too soon.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Jake Tonges
Outlook: For a few weeks in 2025, “Tonges McBasketball” was the hero we didn’t know we needed. With George Kittle nursing his hamstring from Week 2 through Week 6, Tonges stepped up valiantly, giving us at least a couple start-worthy weeks. He had three weeks on the season as the TE9 or better. With Kittle battling back from his Achilles injury, there is a strong chance he won’t be ready to start the year or resemble the same old Kittle when he does. Tonges is practically free in drafts and will be the assumed TE1 on a Shanahan-led team. Consider him a low investment if you need to plug the TE position early.
Dynasty: Tonges was rewarded for his service in 2025 with a two-year contract extension ($8 million) in March. He is the epitome of a strong TE2 with TE1 upside when thrust into the role. Brock Purdy trusts him, and the pass-catchers in San Francisco seem to drop like flies when a light breeze blows through the bay. He could be worth even more to the Kittle manager so feel free to kick the tires there.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Justin Joly
Outlook: “JOE-lee, JOE-lee, JOE-lee, JOE-leeeeee”. All the Dolly Parton fans out there are rejoicing in the correct pronunciation of this fifth-round TE. The converted WR has lower draft capital than we would like, but the team did trade up to take him. Joly had the second-most TDs in his draft class, and his skill with contested catches could make him a solid red zone option for Bo Nix. Do not expect production significant enough to make him relevant in redraft for 2026.
Dynasty: We don’t know how (or if) Joly will fit into Payton’s system behind Evan Engram and Adam Trautman. Joly deserves a taxi-squad spot, knowing he could be the main TE pass-catching option in 2027. Monitor the man.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Ben Sinnott
Outlook: We are a pro-Sinnott pod here, and we are holding on to a wing and a prayer. We loved the draft capital of the second round in 2024, but since joining the league, he has 18 total targets. But, hey… he caught 16 of them! The departure of Zach Ertz piqued our interest again, only to have it smushed like a bug with the signing of Chig Okonkwo. The ultra-athletic Sinnott should sadly not be anywhere near your redraft radar.
Dynasty: All the tea leaves pointed to a potential breakout ala Trey McBride with Zach Ertz and his injury, but it never materialized. Now, with the addition of Chig, any dynasty dream we had of Sinnott being a thing is all but gone. You can cut him and collect your tears if needed.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Noah Fant
Outlook: Noah Fant’s fantasy trajectory has pointed solidly down for years. As a Bengal in 2025, he finished with only 34 receptions and 288 yards in a full rotation. He signed a two-year deal with New Orleans in the off-season, landing behind incumbent starter Juwan Johnson. The team also drafted third-rounder Oscar Delp, making Fant no more than a backup.
Dynasty: Fant was not signed to replace anyone in New Orleans, and his deal was nothing special. The team looks like they are thinking Delp could be their TE of the future. It is a wild fall for a player once taken 20th overall in the NFL Draft in 2019 out of Iowa, aka TE University.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Marlin Klein
Outlook: The Texans really reached on Klein, grabbing the Michigan TE in the second round when most had a Day 3 grade on him. Texans, what do you know that we don’t?! Klein’s college production was lacking, but he did play behind one Mr. LOVEland, so maybe that is somewhat excusable… For redraft purposes, ignore and move on.
Dynasty: We DO have to pay attention to the draft capital, but that doesn’t mean we ignore a college production profile that looks like a dirty diaper. The Doctor is still in his residency for two more years (that is Dalton Schultz, btw), but past him, Klein just needs to bypass the likes of Foster Moreau and Cade Stover. Remember the name, but do not picture a future with Marlin.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Nate Boerkircher
Outlook: Boerkircher (pronounced BOAR-kur-chur) was a surprise second-round selection by the Jaguars. The draft capital is enough to make you pay attention, but when you look back at his performance in college, you are left wanting more - he never topped more than 22 targets in college. Just called him “Boerkircher the Blocker” as that will probably be his main task in Jacksonville.
Dynasty: You have to at least pay attention to the name with Brenton Strange, Quintin Morris, and Hunter Long all due to be free agents come 2027. The Jaguars' TE cupboard will be practically bare. However, the team also drafted Tanner Koziol, who profiles more as the pass-catching TE, suggesting Boerkircher will have to push to be considered in 12 and 13 personnel sets.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Oscar Delp
Outlook: There was a lot of chatter around what could have been with Oscar Delp, but looking at his numbers from college, he never really delivered. He never hit 300 receiving yards at Georgia thanks to competition with some guy named Brock Bowers basically running the offense during Delp’s first two seasons. Even with questions on the production profile, the Saints grabbed him in the third round to add youth to their TE room. He will have a tough time making an impact in redraft this year, presumably buried on the TE depth chart behind Juwan Johnson and Noah Fant.
Dynasty: The athleticism is there, and Delp has the experience in a good college system. That plus draft capital should put him on your dynasty radar as a classic wait-and-see stash. Delp should be taken in your rookie drafts - probably around the TE4/TE5 - and you can sit back and wait to see what Kellen Moore can scheme for the man.
